teamObjective.explanation: With only low spades, the opening lead should be the weakest card to reduce hand clutter and give partner a chance to win the trick with a higher single.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has 3 cards and no public signals have been exchanged, so partner's hand is unknown but likely contains some mid-to-high cards that can beat a 3 of spades.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each have 3 cards and no public actions yet; they probably hold higher cards than 3 and may be able to capture the lead, but their exact strength is uncertain.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades dumps the lowest card, minimizing future liability, while making it easy for partner to win with almost any higher card and keep the initiative for our team.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Playing the 6 uses the strongest card in this weak set; it is still likely beaten by opponents and leaves the 3-5 as dead weight later.
The 4 is only marginally better than the 3 and does not materially change opponent or partner responses, while keeping a lower card in hand.
The 5 is similar to the 4: slightly higher but still low, and saving it offers no clear advantage over leading the 3.

risks: An opponent may play a higher single and seize the lead.
Partner may be unable to beat the 3 if their cards are also low or unsuited.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Even if opponents take the trick, partner still has cards to respond, and retaining 4-6 of spades gives follow-up plays; a low lead is the standard opening strategy.
